content.text: An agentic AI system is not a single artifact — it is a runtime built from dozens of open-source packages, each of which carries its own update history, known vulnerabilities, and maintenance posture. Without a systematic inventory of what is installed and a structured way to measure its risk, security and upgrade decisions become ad hoc: teams patch what they happen to notice and ignore what they do not. Dependency inventory and risk scoring replace that reactive posture with a programmatic, evidence-driven process.

A dependency inventory is a machine-readable, version-complete record of every software component included in a project's build — direct dependencies, transitive dependencies, system libraries, and ML-specific assets such as model weights and ONNX runtimes. A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is the standardised format for this record, expressed in either CycloneDX or SPDX schema and published as part of the build pipeline.

Risk scoring is the process of assigning a numeric priority to each item in the inventory based on its vulnerability status, exploitability, and the business context of the system. The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) provides a baseline score (0–10); a risk scoring model adjusts that baseline with context multipliers specific to the agentic system — for example, whether the affected component is exposed on a public API or whether the agent has access to sensitive user data.
Transitive Dependencies Are the Main Attack Surface
A typical Python agent that imports

has 50 to 100 transitive dependencies after lock-file resolution. Studies of public CVE data consistently show that the majority of vulnerabilities in deployed software reside in transitive, not direct, dependencies — packages the team did not consciously choose and may not know exist. Without a complete inventory, these are invisible.

SOC 2, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, and the US Executive Order on Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity (EO 14028) all require or strongly recommend SBOM publication. Auditors ask for the SBOM to verify that the organisation knows what it has deployed and can account for known vulnerabilities. Without an inventory, compliance evidence must be reconstructed retroactively, which is expensive and error-prone.
Every installed package is known, versioned, and matched against vulnerability databases automatically on every build.
Risk scores direct limited engineering time to the highest-impact vulnerabilities rather than the longest vulnerability list.
A versioned SBOM in the build artifact answers every auditor question about what was deployed and when.
For ML components (model weights, tokenizers), provenance and checksum records allow verification that no tampering occurred between training and deployment.
A prioritised risk list answers the question "which upgrade should we do first?" without requiring ad hoc research each time a Dependabot alert arrives.
Automated scanning on every CI push catches newly disclosed CVEs against the current inventory within hours of the NVD publication, not weeks.

A complete inventory will surface many vulnerabilities, most of which are not exploitable in context. Without risk scoring, teams drown in alerts and prioritise poorly. Risk scoring is what makes the inventory actionable.
Scanners sometimes flag vulnerabilities in packages where the vulnerable code path is not exercised by the application. Triage costs time.
Standard SBOM tooling was designed for software, not for model weights. Tracking weights and runtime artefacts requires supplementary processes.
Context multipliers in the risk model must be kept current as the architecture evolves. A multiplier calibrated when the agent was internal-only will understate risk after it becomes internet-facing.
The inventory process runs in three stages. Discovery scans the project manifest files (

) and resolves transitive dependencies from the lock file to produce a complete package list. Scoring cross-references each package and version against public vulnerability databases (OSV, NVD, GitHub Advisory Database) and computes a risk score using CVSS base scores adjusted by context multipliers. Publication exports the resulting SBOM in CycloneDX or SPDX format and pushes it to the build artifact registry and an internal vulnerability dashboard.

Context multipliers are the layer that makes CVSS useful in practice. A vulnerability with CVSS 7.5 in a dependency that is only loaded during offline batch processing of non-sensitive data is much less urgent than the same score in a dependency that sits in the HTTP request path of an internet-facing agent with access to user PII. The multiplier adjusts the effective priority accordingly.

attrs.alt: Dependency inventory architecture: automated discovery feeds three columns (direct dependencies, transitive dependencies, ML components) into a CVSS and context-multiplier risk scoring layer, which produces an SBOM and a vulnerability dashboard with alerting.

content.text: The top layer runs automated discovery on every CI push. Direct, transitive, and ML-specific components are catalogued separately because they have different ownership responsibilities and update cadences. All three feed the risk scoring engine, which applies CVSS base scores plus context multipliers (internet-facing, data scope, control coverage) to produce an adjusted priority. The adjusted scores determine SLA tier and feed two outputs: the SBOM published to the artifact registry, and the vulnerability dashboard that alerts on P0 and P1 findings.
Dependency scanning should be a standard CI step that fails the build on any newly discovered P0 or P1 vulnerability, the same as a failing unit test.
An SBOM should be generated and published as part of the release artifact, capturing a point-in-time inventory of exactly what was shipped.
A new tool, database, or external API added to the agent changes the attack surface. Recalibrate context multipliers when the deployment model changes.
Auditors request the SBOM. Having it generated automatically on every build means it is always available and always current.
A major version bump in a core library can pull in a subtly different transitive graph. Regenerate and diff the SBOM to surface any newly introduced packages.
A complete, reproducible inventory requires a lock file. For Python, generate one with

# Generate a pinned lock file from high-level requirements
pip-compile requirements.in --generate-hashes --output-file requirements.txt

# Install from lock file in CI to ensure reproducibility
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.txt

# Verify no new packages were installed beyond what is in the lock file
pip-audit -r requirements.txt --output json > sbom_candidates.json

# Using cyclonedx-python to produce a CycloneDX SBOM
pip install cyclonedx-bom
cyclonedx-py environment --output-format json > sbom.json

# Alternatively, using syft for a multi-language SBOM
# (works for Python, Node, Go, container images)
syft scan . -o cyclonedx-json > sbom.json

# Publish to build artifact storage (e.g. GitHub release assets)
gh release upload v1.4.2 sbom.json#"SBOM (CycloneDX)"

# .github/workflows/sbom-and-audit.yml
name: SBOM and Vulnerability Audit
on:
  push:
    branches: ['**']
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'  # nightly scan for newly disclosed CVEs

jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: '3.12', cache: pip}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt cyclonedx-bom pip-audit
      - name: Generate SBOM
        run: cyclonedx-py environment -o json > sbom-${{ github.sha }}.json
      - name: Upload SBOM as artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: sbom-${{ github.sha }}
          path: sbom-${{ github.sha }}.json
      - name: Vulnerability scan
        run: |
          pip-audit -r requirements.txt --output json > audit.json
          python scripts/risk_score.py audit.json  # exit 1 on P0/P1
      - name: Alert on critical findings
        if: failure()
        run: |
          echo "Critical vulnerability found — triggering PagerDuty"
          curl -X POST "$PAGERDUTY_WEBHOOK" -d '{"event":"p0_vulnerability"}'
        env:
          PAGERDUTY_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.PAGERDUTY_WEBHOOK }}
SBOM Diff After a Dependency Upgrade

# Compare SBOMs before and after a major version bump to surface new packages
pip install sbom-diff  # hypothetical; real tools include diffoscope, cdxgen, etc.

# Or using a Python one-liner with two JSON SBOMs:
python3 << 'EOF'
import json

before = {c['name'] + '==' + c['version']
          for c in json.load(open('sbom_before.json'))['components']}
after  = {c['name'] + '==' + c['version']
          for c in json.load(open('sbom_after.json'))['components']}

added   = after - before
removed = before - after

print("Added packages:",   '\n  '.join(sorted(added))   or 'none')
print("Removed packages:", '\n  '.join(sorted(removed)) or 'none')
EOF
A multi-tenant agentic document-processing service had 78 direct dependencies and, after lock-file resolution, 312 installed packages. Running

on the initial inventory surfaced 41 vulnerabilities across 19 packages. Without risk scoring, the team would have needed to triage all 41 manually. With the context-aware scoring model (internet-facing: true, PII access: true), the adjusted scores clustered the vulnerabilities into: 3 at P0, 7 at P1, 22 at P2, and 9 at P3.

The team resolved the 3 P0 vulnerabilities in the same week using the emergency patch procedure. The 7 P1 vulnerabilities were scheduled in the next two-week sprint. The 22 P2 vulnerabilities became backlog items with a 7-day SLA each. The 9 P3 vulnerabilities were tracked but deprioritised. Total remediation time for the critical tail: one sprint, versus the months-long backlog the team had previously accumulated with an unscored vulnerability list.
(locked) misses transitive dependencies and reports incomplete results.
Treating CVSS base score as the final priority.
A CVSS 9.0 vulnerability in a package that is only used in a CLI utility run locally by a developer has a very different real-world risk than the same score in an internet-facing API path.
Model weights, tokenizer vocabularies, and ONNX runtimes are not in
but can be tampered with (supply-chain poisoning). Track their checksums separately.
Generating the SBOM only at release time.
An SBOM generated only at release captures a snapshot that quickly becomes stale. Generate on every CI push to ensure the inventory reflects the current state.
As the system evolves (adding public endpoints, new data access patterns), the risk model must be updated. Stale multipliers lead to incorrect priority assignments.
Sending every vulnerability to the same Slack channel trains teams to ignore the channel. Route P0/P1 to PagerDuty, P2/P3 to a weekly digest email.
Commit the lock file to version control.
) must be committed so the inventory is reproducible across all environments.
Include hash verification in CI installs.
ensures that what is installed matches what was audited, closing the supply-chain gap between audit and install.
Publish SBOMs as immutable build artifacts.
Attach the SBOM to each tagged release in the artifact registry. This provides the auditor-accessible record of what was deployed at each version.
Review the context multiplier model quarterly.
Schedule a lightweight review of the risk model at the same time as the quarterly security review. Update multipliers whenever the system's deployment scope changes.
For every model weight file or tokenizer artifact, record the SHA-256 hash in a manifest committed to the repository. Verify on load and alert on mismatch.
Track the average time from vulnerability discovery to resolution by priority tier. Use this metric in security reviews to demonstrate programme maturity and identify systemic delays.
Dependency scanning adds time to CI. A typical
run against 300 installed packages takes 15–30 seconds with a warm network cache (OSV database is fetched once per CI runner lifetime). SBOM generation with
takes 5–10 seconds. Total overhead is under one minute, which is acceptable in a pipeline that already runs tests.
). Cache it as a CI artefact refreshed nightly to avoid repeated downloads on every push.
Run full scans on a schedule, incremental scans on push.
On push, scan only the direct dependency graph (fast). Nightly, scan the full transitive graph against the latest database updates (thorough).
Use a dedicated vulnerability management platform for large inventories.
At scale (500+ packages across multiple services), tools like Snyk, Grype, or Dependency-Track provide a centralised dashboard with deduplication, suppression management, and trend tracking.
appears in five microservices, it should produce one remediation ticket, not five. A centralised platform handles this; a per-repo scan does not.
The standard Python dependency auditor, backed by OSV. Outputs JSON, CSV, or table format. Run as
Generates deterministic lock files from high-level requirements. The lock file is the foundation of a complete inventory.
Generates CycloneDX-format SBOMs from a Python environment. Supports JSON and XML output.
Multi-language SBOM generator that works on source directories, Python environments, container images, and OCI archives. Outputs CycloneDX or SPDX.
Vulnerability scanner that consumes Syft SBOMs and matches them against multiple databases (NVD, OSV, GitHub Advisory).
GitHub's automated dependency update service. Opens pull requests for outdated or vulnerable packages. Configure with
to set update frequency and allowed reviewers.

Commercial platform with richer prioritisation (exploitability data, reachability analysis), PR-level scanning, and a centralised dashboard. Particularly useful for large multi-service agentic platforms.
Open-source platform for SBOM ingestion, vulnerability tracking, policy enforcement, and trend dashboards. Self-hosted; integrates with most CI systems.

plus Dependabot provides adequate coverage at zero cost. For production platforms with multiple services and ML components, a commercial tool (Snyk) or a self-hosted platform (Dependency-Track) adds the cross-service deduplication, reachability analysis, and policy enforcement needed to operate the programme at scale.
Verify model weight checksums on every container startup.
A model weight file tampered between training and deployment is invisible to a software SBOM. Compute the SHA-256 of each weight file at startup and compare against the committed manifest.
Use hash pinning in the lock file.
adds SHA-256 hashes for every package wheel.
verifies them on install, blocking supply-chain substitution attacks.
Treat the SBOM as a security artifact.
The SBOM reveals the full attack surface of the deployed system. Restrict access to SBOMs that include internal package names or version information that could assist an attacker.
Do not suppress findings without a documented exception.
Scanners allow suppression of false positives. Every suppressed finding must have a written rationale, an expiry date, and an approving engineer. Undocumented suppression creates hidden risk.

If the set of installed packages in production differs from the SBOM generated at release time, that is an integrity signal. Alert on any discrepancy between the deployed image's package list and the release SBOM.

A dependency inventory is a complete, version-pinned record of all software components in the build, including transitive dependencies and ML assets — not just the packages the team directly imported.

CVSS base scores alone are insufficient for prioritisation; context multipliers (internet-facing, PII access, presence of mitigating controls) convert CVSS into an actionable risk tier matched to a defined SLA.
Generate and publish an SBOM in CycloneDX or SPDX format on every CI build, attaching it to each tagged release as an immutable compliance artifact.
Pin every dependency with a lock file and use hash verification on install to close the supply-chain gap between the audited inventory and what actually runs in production.
Track ML components (model weights, tokenizer files) separately from software packages, with SHA-256 checksums verified at container startup.
Maintain a MTTP KPI by priority tier and review it in quarterly security reviews to surface systemic delays in the remediation programme.
For large multi-service platforms, a centralised vulnerability management tool (Snyk, Dependency-Track) is essential for deduplication, suppression management, and cross-service trend tracking.
